statement.markdown: You are given a sorted array that was initially filled with the same number, but one of its elements was changed to a different number. The array is sorted in ascending order, but the changed element disrupts this order. Find the element that was changed. The array will contain at least 3 elements and at most 10000 elements. The elements will be in the range [1, 10000].
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer, the element that was changed.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

hints.text: The array is sorted in ascending order, and one element is changed to a different number. Think about how you can use this information to find the changed element.
Since the array is sorted, you can use a binary search approach to find the changed element. Consider how you can use the middle element to narrow down the search space.
Think about how you can use the fact that the array is sorted to your advantage. You can use the fact that the middle element is either the smallest or the second smallest element in the array.
Consider how you can use the fact that the changed element disrupts the order of the array. You can use this information to determine which half of the array the changed element is likely to be in.

The key to solving this problem is to use a binary search approach, where you repeatedly divide the search space in half until you find the changed element. Think about how you can use the fact that the array is sorted and the changed element disrupts the order to make this approach work.

editorial.title: Single Element in Sorted Array
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach is correct but slow because it checks every element in the array to see if it appears only once, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2) due to the use of the count method inside a loop.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution works by iterating over each element in the array and using the count method to check if the element appears only once, but this is inefficient for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient solution would involve using a hash map or dictionary to store the frequency of each element, allowing for a single pass through the array.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal approach involves using a hash map to count the frequency of each element in a single pass, then iterating over the map to find the element with a count of 1, resulting in a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it correctly handles all possible cases, including when the single element is at the start, end, or middle of the array, and when there are duplicate elements.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution would involve creating a sample array, counting the frequency of each element using a hash map, and then finding the element with a count of 1.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the single element is at the start or end of the array
Not using a hash map to count the frequency of each element
Using the count method inside a loop, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2)
Not checking for the case where there are no single elements in the array
Not handling the case where the array is empty
